concepts.name: Drugs and Medicines – Definition and Chemotherapy
concepts.explanation: Drugs are chemicals of low molecular mass (~100-500 u) that interact with macromolecular targets to produce a biological response. When the response is therapeutic and useful, they are called medicines. The use of chemicals for therapeutic effect is called chemotherapy.

concepts.name: Classification of Drugs – Pharmacological Effect
concepts.explanation: This classification is based on the pharmacological effect of the drug (what it does to the body). It is useful for doctors as it provides the whole range of drugs available for treating a particular problem. Examples: analgesics (pain killers), antiseptics (kill microorganisms).

concepts.name: Classification of Drugs – Drug Action
concepts.explanation: Based on the action of a drug on a particular biochemical process. For example, antihistamines inhibit the action of histamine, which causes inflammation. Different drugs can block histamine action in various ways.

concepts.name: Classification of Drugs – Chemical Structure
concepts.explanation: Based on the chemical structure of the drug. Drugs classified this way share common structural features and often have similar pharmacological activity. Example: sulphonamides share a common structural feature.

concepts.name: Classification of Drugs – Molecular Targets
concepts.explanation: Drugs interact with biomolecules such as carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids (target molecules). This classification is the most useful for medicinal chemists as it focuses on the mechanism of action.

concepts.name: Enzymes as Drug Targets – Catalytic Action
concepts.explanation: Enzymes catalyse reactions by: (i) holding the substrate at the active site through ionic, hydrogen, van der Waals, or dipole-dipole interactions; (ii) providing functional groups that attack the substrate. Drugs inhibit these activities.

concepts.name: Drug-Enzyme Interaction – Competitive Inhibition
concepts.explanation: Competitive inhibitors compete with the natural substrate for attachment to the active site of the enzyme. They block the binding site and prevent the substrate from binding, inhibiting the enzyme's catalytic activity.

concepts.name: Drug-Enzyme Interaction – Non-Competitive Inhibition
concepts.explanation: Non-competitive inhibitors bind to a different site on the enzyme called the allosteric site. This changes the shape of the active site so that the substrate cannot recognise it. If the bond is covalent, the enzyme is permanently blocked.

concepts.name: Receptors as Drug Targets
concepts.explanation: Receptors are proteins embedded in cell membranes that are crucial to the body's communication process. Chemical messengers bind to receptor sites, changing their shape and transmitting messages into the cell without entering it.

concepts.name: Antagonists and Agonists
concepts.explanation: Antagonists are drugs that bind to receptor sites and inhibit their natural function (blocking messages). Agonists mimic natural messengers and switch on the receptor (useful when there is a lack of natural chemical messenger).

concepts.name: Antacids – Cimetidine and Ranitidine
concepts.explanation: Antacids neutralise excess stomach acid. Cimetidine (Tegamet) and ranitidine (Zantac) prevent the interaction of histamine with receptors in the stomach wall, reducing acid secretion. They are more effective than simple metal hydroxides as they treat the cause, not just symptoms.

concepts.name: Antihistamines
concepts.explanation: Histamine is a vasodilator responsible for nasal congestion and allergic responses. Antihistamines (e.g., brompheniramine, terfenadine) compete with histamine for binding sites on receptors. Antiallergic and antacid drugs work on different receptors, hence they do not interfere with each other.

concepts.name: Tranquilizers (Antidepressants)
concepts.explanation: Tranquilizers are neurologically active drugs used to treat stress, anxiety, and mental diseases. They affect the message transfer mechanism from nerve to receptor. Examples: chlordiazepoxide, meprobamate (mild tranquilizers), equanil (depression and hypertension).

concepts.name: Antidepressants – Noradrenaline Mechanism
concepts.explanation: Noradrenaline is a neurotransmitter that plays a role in mood changes. Low levels cause depression. Antidepressant drugs inhibit enzymes that catalyse the degradation of noradrenaline, allowing it to activate receptors for longer. Examples: iproniazid, phenelzine.

concepts.name: Antimicrobials – Antibiotics
concepts.explanation: Antibiotics are substances produced wholly or partly by chemical synthesis that inhibit or destroy microorganisms at low concentrations. They can be bactericidal (killing) or bacteriostatic (inhibiting). Broad-spectrum antibiotics affect a wide range of bacteria; narrow-spectrum affect specific types.

concepts.name: Antibiotics – Penicillin and Sulpha Drugs
concepts.explanation: Penicillin, discovered by Alexander Fleming, was the first effective antibacterial agent. Sulpha drugs (sulphonamides) were developed from prontosil, which is converted to sulphanilamide in the body. Examples: penicillin G (narrow spectrum), ampicillin, amoxycillin (broad spectrum), chloramphenicol.

concepts.name: Antiseptics and Disinfectants
concepts.explanation: Antiseptics are applied to living tissues (wounds, cuts) to kill or prevent growth of microorganisms. Examples: dettol (chloroxylenol + terpineol), bithionol (in soaps), tincture of iodine (2-3% iodine in alcohol-water). Disinfectants are applied to inanimate objects (floors, instruments). The same substance can act as both at different concentrations (e.g., phenol 0.2% antiseptic, 1% disinfectant).

concepts.name: Antifertility Drugs (Birth Control Pills)
concepts.explanation: Birth control pills contain a mixture of synthetic estrogen and progesterone derivatives. Progesterone suppresses ovulation. Norethindrone is a synthetic progesterone derivative; ethynylestradiol is the estrogen derivative used in combination.

concepts.name: Artificial Sweetening Agents
concepts.explanation: Artificial sweeteners are used as sugar substitutes to reduce calorie intake. Examples: saccharin (first popular, 550 times sweeter than sugar), aspartame (100 times, methyl ester of aspartic acid-phenylalanine dipeptide, unstable at cooking temperature), sucralose (trichloro derivative of sucrose, stable at cooking temperature), alitame (high potency).

concepts.name: Food Preservatives
concepts.explanation: Food preservatives prevent spoilage of food due to microbial growth. Examples: table salt, sugar, vegetable oils, sodium benzoate (C₆H₅COONa, metabolised in the body), salts of sorbic acid, and propanoic acid.

concepts.name: Antioxidants in Food
concepts.explanation: Antioxidants retard the action of oxygen on food, helping in preservation. They are more reactive towards oxygen than the food material. Examples: BHT (butylated hydroxytoluene), BHA (butylated hydroxyanisole), sulphur dioxide, sulphites (for wine, beer, dried fruits).

concepts.name: Soaps – Saponification
concepts.explanation: Soaps are sodium or potassium salts of long-chain fatty acids (stearic, oleic, palmitic acids). They are prepared by the saponification of fats/oils (glyceryl esters) with aqueous NaOH or KOH. Glycerol is a by-product.

concepts.name: Types of Soaps
concepts.explanation: Toilet soaps use better grades of fats/oils with excess alkali removed; colours and perfumes added. Floating soaps have air bubbles; transparent soaps are made by dissolving soap in ethanol. Medicated soaps have medicinal substances. Shaving soaps contain glycerol. Laundry soaps contain fillers (sodium rosinate, silicate, borax, carbonate).

concepts.name: Soaps and Hard Water – Problem
concepts.explanation: Hard water contains Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions. These form insoluble calcium and magnesium soaps (scum) with sodium or potassium soaps: 2C₁₇H₃₅COONa + CaCl₂ → (C₁₇H₃₅COO)₂Ca + 2NaCl. This scum adheres to fabric, making soaps ineffective in hard water.

concepts.name: Synthetic Detergents – Introduction
concepts.explanation: Synthetic detergents are cleansing agents that have all the properties of soaps but do not contain soap. They work in both soft and hard water and give foam even in cold water. They are classified as anionic, cationic, and non-ionic.

concepts.name: Anionic Detergents
concepts.explanation: Anionic detergents are sodium salts of sulphonated long-chain alcohols or hydrocarbons. The anionic part is involved in cleansing. Examples: sodium alkyl sulphates (from long-chain alcohols + H₂SO₄), sodium alkylbenzene sulphonates. Used in household work and toothpastes.

concepts.name: Cationic Detergents
concepts.explanation: Cationic detergents are quaternary ammonium salts of amines with acetates, chlorides, or bromides as anions. The cationic part has a long hydrocarbon chain and a positive charge on nitrogen. Example: cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (used in hair conditioners). They have germicidal properties but are expensive.

concepts.name: Non-Ionic Detergents
concepts.explanation: Non-ionic detergents do not contain any ions. Example: stearic acid reacts with polyethylene glycol to form a non-ionic detergent. Liquid dishwashing detergents are non-ionic. Cleansing action is the same as soaps (micelle formation).

concepts.name: Biodegradable and Non-Biodegradable Detergents
concepts.explanation: Detergents with highly branched hydrocarbon chains are non-biodegradable (bacteria cannot degrade them), leading to accumulation and pollution. Unbranched chains are biodegradable. Modern detergents have controlled branching to minimise environmental pollution.
